turns.content: Hey, I've been reading about partitioning iterators in Rust but still don't fully understand how to actually collect partial results. I'm working on a side project over the weekend and trying to handle failures gracefully without losing good data.

Hey there! It's totally normal to get tripped up by that because Java's try-catch blocks make it feel like you have to stop entirely. In Rust, the goal is usually to separate successes from failures while keeping everything in one place.

Right, so I was thinking if I just use filter_map on my iterator of Results, that should give me a flat list where only successful items make it through. Is that the right approach for partial recovery?

You're close but there's a subtle distinction here. Filter_map will drop failures entirely from your output, which means you can't recover them later or log them separately. For partial results where you want both the successes and the failure records intact, partition is actually the better tool.
Oh okay, so partition keeps everything around? Like I get two separate collections instead of one filtered list?

Exactly. Partition takes an iterator and splits it into two iterators based on a predicate function. You can then collect both iterators independently using flat_map or similar methods to build your final structures.
Got it, so I'm building two Vecs instead of one? One for successes and one for failures?

That's exactly right. You can collect the success iterator into a Vec<Result<T>> where all are Ok values, or map them to their inner T types, while collecting the failure iterator separately so you don't lose those error logs.
And if I used filter_map instead, would I still be able to access the failures later?
No, filter_map discards anything that returns None or Err. You lose that data permanently unless you pre-collect it elsewhere before filtering.
Makes sense, so partition is the way to go when I need both success and failure records in my final output?
Precisely. It's especially useful when you want to log failures separately or aggregate them later without breaking your main data flow.
Alright, so the pattern is: partition my iterator into successes and failures, then collect each part with map or flat_map as needed. Thanks for clarifying this!
You're welcome! That's a solid mental model to have for Rust error handling when you want partial data recovery.
